items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the speech beginning 'All the world's a stage, / And all the men and women merely players'?
items.explanation:
Act 2, Scene 7; spoken by the melancholy Jacques (pronounced 'JAY-kwiz') in the Forest of Arden
The 'Seven Ages of Man' speech catalogues life from infant to second childhood: 'all the men and women merely players'
Jaques is a minor character whose cynicism provides counterpoint to the play's romantic optimism
The Globe Theatre's name may be an allusion to this very line

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the speech beginning 'Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears'?
items.explanation:
Act 3, Scene 2; Mark Antony's funeral oration, delivered immediately after Brutus's cold, logical speech
Antony uses devastating irony — 'Brutus is an honorable man' — to turn the Roman crowd against the conspirators
The speech is a textbook example of rhetorical technique: ethos, pathos, and logos deployed in sequence
Antony wins the crowd by showing Caesar's wounds and reading his will, which leaves money to every Roman citizen
items.cardFront: "Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears"

links.text: "Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the line 'Et tu, Brute?'
items.explanation:
Act 3, Scene 1; Caesar's last words as Brutus delivers the final stab
The phrase has become the ultimate byword for betrayal by a trusted friend
Historical sources (Suetonius) give Caesar's actual dying words in Greek: 'καὶ σύ, τέκνον' ('You too, child') — possibly addressed to Brutus as a rumored illegitimate son
Brutus's betrayal is the crux: Caesar covered his face with his toga when he saw Brutus raise his dagger

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play opens with the line 'Now is the winter of our discontent / Made glorious summer by this sun of York'?
items.explanation:
Act 1, Scene 1; Richard's opening soliloquy, immediately establishing him as gleeful schemer
The line is often misquoted in isolation — the complete sentence is actually celebratory: 'our discontent' has ended
'Sun of York' is a pun: the sun was Edward IV's heraldic emblem; Richard resents living in his brother's shadow
Shakespeare's Richard III is largely Tudor propaganda — portraying him as a deformed monster; historians debate the historical Richard's character
items.cardFront: "Now is the winter of our discontent"

links.text: "Now is the winter of our discontent"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the lines 'What's in a name? That which we call a rose / By any other name would smell as sweet'?
items.explanation:
Act 2, Scene 2 (the balcony scene); Juliet argues that Romeo's family name is irrelevant to his worth as a person
Central to the play's theme: names and social identity (Montague vs. Capulet) vs. personal identity and love
'Romeo' has become generic English for any passionate young male lover
Based on Arthur Brooke's 1562 poem *The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet*, itself derived from Italian sources
items.cardFront: "What's in a name? That which we call a rose..."

links.text: "What's in a name? That which we call a rose..."

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the line 'All that glitters is not gold'?
items.explanation:
Act 2, Scene 7; the line appears on a scroll inside the gold casket opened by the Prince of Morocco
Original First Folio text: 'All that glisters is not gold' — 'glisters' was later modernized to 'glitters'
Morocco chose the gold casket based on surface appearance; the scroll mocks his superficiality
The casket test (gold, silver, lead) is a central plot device of the Portia subplot

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the speech beginning 'The quality of mercy is not strained, / It droppeth as the gentle rain from heaven'?
items.explanation:
Act 4, Scene 1; spoken by Portia, disguised as a young lawyer named Balthasar, urging Shylock to show mercy
Portia goes on to defeat Shylock on a technicality: his bond specifies flesh but not blood
The speech articulates a Christian theology of mercy over strict legal justice
Shakespeare's longest female speaking role; Portia also solves the 'ring test' subplot
items.cardFront: "The quality of mercy is not strained"

links.text: "The quality of mercy is not strained"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the advice 'This above all: to thine own self be true'?
items.explanation:
Act 1, Scene 3; Polonius's parting advice to his son Laertes departing for France
Ironically, Polonius is one of Shakespeare's most hypocritical and meddlesome characters — the advice is given without self-awareness
Continues: 'And it must follow, as the night the day, / Thou canst not then be false to any man'
Frequently quoted out of context as universal wisdom; within the play it is undermined by its speaker's own conduct
items.cardFront: "This above all: to thine own self be true"

links.text: "This above all: to thine own self be true"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the line 'Something is rotten in the state of Denmark'?
items.explanation:
Act 1, Scene 4; spoken by Marcellus the guard — NOT by Hamlet (a common misattribution)
Said after the ghost of King Hamlet appears and beckons Hamlet away from the guards into the darkness
Captures the play's central theme: Denmark's court is corrupted by Claudius's fratricide
'The state of Denmark' has become shorthand in English for political or institutional corruption
items.cardFront: "Something is rotten in the state of Denmark"

links.text: "Something is rotten in the state of Denmark"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play opens with the line 'If music be the food of love, play on'?
items.explanation:
Act 1, Scene 1; spoken by Duke Orsino, pining self-indulgently for the Countess Olivia
Continues: 'Give me excess of it, that, surfeiting, / The appetite may sicken, and so die'
Orsino is in love with the idea of love; he eventually falls for Viola (disguised as Cesario)
The play's title refers to Twelfth Night (Epiphany, January 6) — a holiday associated with misrule, disguise, and revelry
items.cardFront: "If music be the food of love, play on"

links.text: "If music be the food of love, play on"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the lines 'We are such stuff / As dreams are made on, and our little life / Is rounded with a sleep'?
items.explanation:
Act 4, Scene 1; Prospero, after abruptly dismissing a masque of spirits, meditates on the insubstantiality of life
Not 'made of' but 'made on' — the preposition is frequently misquoted
Widely read as Shakespeare's own theatrical farewell — *The Tempest* was likely his last solo-authored play
In *The Maltese Falcon* (1941), Sam Spade paraphrases it: 'the stuff that dreams are made of'
items.cardFront: "We are such stuff as dreams are made on"

links.text: "We are such stuff as dreams are made on"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the rallying cry 'Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more'?
items.explanation:
Act 3, Scene 1; Henry's speech before the assault on the walls of Harfleur
'Breach' refers to a gap in the city walls; 'close the wall up with our English dead' follows
The play also contains the 'St. Crispin's Day' speech ('We few, we happy few, we band of brothers') before Agincourt
Kenneth Branagh delivered the speech memorably in his celebrated 1989 film adaptation
items.cardFront: "Once more unto the breach, dear friends"

links.text: "Once more unto the breach, dear friends"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the line 'The lady doth protest too much, methinks'?
items.explanation:
Act 3, Scene 2; spoken by Gertrude, Hamlet's mother, watching the Player Queen in the play-within-a-play
'Protest' in Elizabethan English means 'to solemnly declare' — the line means the queen is making her vows too emphatically, not objecting too loudly
Often misquoted as 'protests too much' — the original has 'doth'
Ironically, Gertrude herself is the woman who remarried with suspicious haste; Hamlet staged the play to expose Claudius
items.cardFront: "The lady doth protest too much, methinks"

links.text: "The lady doth protest too much, methinks"

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the sleepwalking scene in which a character cries 'Out, damned spot! out, I say!'?
items.explanation:
Act 5, Scene 1; Lady Macbeth sleepwalking, tormented by guilt over Duncan's murder
She imagines blood on her hands that cannot be washed away — reversing her earlier boast 'A little water clears us of this deed'
A doctor and gentlewoman observe the scene; her ravings inadvertently confess the murders
Among Shakespeare's most powerful portrayals of guilt consuming a once-ruthless character

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the line 'A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!'?
items.explanation:
Act 5, Scene 4; Richard at the Battle of Bosworth Field (1485), after his horse is killed in combat
Richard is killed moments later by Henry Tudor's forces — the last English king to die in battle
The Battle of Bosworth ended the Wars of the Roses; Richard III was the last Plantagenet king
In 2012, Richard III's skeleton was discovered beneath a Leicester car park; DNA confirmed the identification in 2013
items.cardFront: "A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!"

links.text: "A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!"
